headline: ICE detention cases begin weaving through North Dakota courts
summary: Seven emergency petitions challenging ICE detentions were filed in North Dakota federal court in the past year, all in the last three months, as ICE arrests in the state rose from 81 to 202 year-over-year; a new DHS policy eliminating bond hearings for most noncitizens detained inside the country has produced conflicting rulings from two North Dakota federal judges—Judge Daniel Hovland ordered hearings while Judge Dan Traynor denied petitions and deferred to the executive branch's interpretation; the surge in enforcement and legal uncertainty has prompted local attorney Sue Swanson to train more North Dakota lawyers and nonprofits to advise immigrants on precautionary measures.

headline: Crow, Neguse visit Aurora ICE facility after court ruling restored right to unannounced visits
summary: Colorado Reps. Jason Crow and Joe Neguse conducted an unannounced oversight visit to the Aurora ICE detention center after a federal judge temporarily blocked a Trump administration policy requiring seven days' notice for congressional inspections, which conflicts with 2019 appropriations law granting immediate access. Neguse, lead plaintiff in the lawsuit challenging the policy, joined Crow—previously denied entry in July—to inspect the GEO Group-operated facility currently holding 1,376 detainees. The visit occurs amid heightened scrutiny of immigration enforcement following 32 reported deaths in ICE custody in 2025 and concerns over transparency and accountability in detention operations.

headline: Workers vote to authorize strike at Colorado’s JBS meatpacking plant
summary: Over 99% of the 3,800 United Food and Commercial Workers Local 7 members at Greeley's JBS meatpacking plant voted to authorize an unfair labor practices strike following eight months of failed contract negotiations. The union alleges JBS violated federal labor laws by refusing to bargain in good faith and engaging in worker intimidation and retaliation. Employees also report dangerous conditions from increased processing speeds and reduced hours. While no strike date has been set, the vote escalates labor unrest at one of America's largest meatpacking facilities. UFCW Local 7 President Kim Cordova stated JBS must cease its illegal practices and negotiate fairly or face the consequences.

headline: Morrisey appoints Baylous to lead WV Homeland Security
summary: West Virginia Gov. Patrick Morrisey appointed former U.S. Marshal Michael Baylous as state Department of Homeland Security secretary, a role that could involve coordinating with federal immigration enforcement agencies and managing state-level responses to immigration-related public safety matters.

headline: After Minneapolis immigration crackdown, Birkebeiner director says foreign skiers express concerns
summary: International participants in Wisconsin's American Birkebeiner ski race have raised safety concerns about traveling through Minneapolis after recent federal immigration enforcement actions, including the shooting deaths of two Minneapolis residents by immigration agents. Foreign skiers from 25 countries are questioning whether to attend, with one requesting a race contract number in case of immigration stops, while organizers reassure them but acknowledge potential economic impacts. The concerns reflect broader drops in international tourism following the Trump administration's "American First" immigration crackdown and suspension of visa programs.

headline: Twin Cities ICE presence extends into Wisconsin
summary: ICE agents from Minnesota conducted raids in Wisconsin communities of Hudson and Baldwin, arresting immigrants at the St. Croix County Courthouse and a local restaurant, with up to 50 households in the Baldwin-Woodville school district losing a parent; the operations have strained community-police relations amid reports of Baldwin police cooperating with ICE, while the St. Croix County Sheriff's Department maintains a policy protecting crime victims but allows notifying ICE about undocumented detainees, deepening political divisions in the rural area.

headline: Rock Springs senator seeks lengthy prison sentences for impeding federal agents
summary: Sen. John Kolb (R-Rock Springs) is drafting legislation that would make it a felony punishable by 5-25 years in prison to impede federal immigration enforcement agents like ICE, a response to a January incident in Rock Springs where protesters blocked ICE agents at a hotel. The proposed penalty would exceed the maximum sentences for aggravated vehicular homicide and manslaughter, and would carry a mandatory minimum. As a non-budgetary measure, the bill would require a two-thirds majority vote to be introduced in the upcoming legislative session starting Feb. 9. Kolb, who serves on the Senate Judiciary Committee, stated his intent to distinguish Wyoming from other states like Minnesota and expressed strong support for law enforcement. Sen. Tara Nethercott (R-Cheyenne) is separately exploring enhancements to existing interference with peace officer laws to address similar concerns.

headline: Wyoming’s 2026 budget session starts Monday. Here’s what you need to know.
summary: The Wyoming Legislature's 2026 two-year budget process centers on controversial cuts proposed by the Joint Appropriations Committee—defunding the Wyoming Business Council, halting Wyoming Public Media support, and slashing health and university funding—driven by the newly majority-holding Freedom Caucus's political agenda to reshape government rather than address deficits. Gov. Mark Gordon's $11.1 billion "essentials" proposal faces substantial revision despite his expressed concerns, though his constitutional authority is limited to line-item vetoes. With Freedom Caucus control of the House, budget passage depends on inter-chamber negotiations and potential conflict with the executive branch. The legislature must reconcile likely divergent House and Senate versions within its four-week session to meet constitutional requirements. The outcome will test the balance between the caucus's government-reshaping goals and traditional state spending priorities.

headline: Lawmakers will tackle public school funding, literacy, cell phones, student discipline
summary: The Wyoming Legislature is undertaking a court-mandated recalibration of its K-12 public school funding model, which will impact teacher salaries, class sizes, and support services like mental health counseling. Educators and superintendents have criticized the draft bill for increasing class sizes, eliminating hundreds of teaching positions, reducing local control, and inadequately addressing court-ordered mandates for student support services. Additional legislation includes Senate File 59 establishing evidence-based literacy instruction, Senate File 35 requiring cell phone/smartwatch policies, and House Bill 65 reforming student discipline by repealing corporal punishment immunity. Lawmakers will also consider bills allowing homeschool students to participate in public school extracurriculars (HB 23), mandating suicide prevention education (HB 40), expanding Hathaway scholarships to private institutions (HB 57), and requiring schools to provide feminine hygiene products (HB 74). The session's 20-day timeline adds urgency to these comprehensive K-12 policy changes.

headline: House panel advances bills limiting ICE activity in Virginia
summary: A Virginia House subcommittee advanced Democratic bills to restrict ICE operations by requiring judicial warrants for courthouse arrests and limiting enforcement near polling places and schools. The measures would reduce cooperation between state-local law enforcement and federal immigration authorities, penalize officers who conceal identities or impersonate agents, and follow Gov. Spanberger's executive order ending state police assistance to ICE. The action responds to recent reports of masked ICE agents conducting warrantless arrests at courthouses.

headline: Measles case reported in Northern Virginia adult and more state headlines
summary: A measles case in Northern Virginia and the reopening of a Boar's Head plant linked to a fatal listeria outbreak highlight the role of state disease surveillance and food safety regulation.

headline: Reykdal makes pitch for spending income tax collections on education
summary: Washington Superintendent Chris Reykdal proposes allocating $861 million annually from a newly proposed 9.9% income tax on incomes over $1 million to K-12 education, including $306 million for special education, $150 million for universal school meals, and $147 million for materials and operations, while warning of a constitutional crisis over compliance with the state Supreme Court's basic education funding mandate; the plan also includes cutting $1.4 billion in property taxes that currently fund schools, pending legislative approval and a likely voter ballot before generating revenue in 2029.

headline: Southern Nevada activists organize ICE response network
summary: LV Defensa, a Southern Nevada grassroots rapid response network formed in October 2025, has surged by hundreds of members following Minneapolis CBP killings and models itself after California's VC Defensa to monitor and resist ICE activity through legal observation and community alerts. The organization coordinates with the Nevada Immigrant Coalition and has supported detainees like Emine Sahin, who was held for 122 days while pregnant in Henderson Detention Center before being transferred to ICE custody after criminal charges were dropped. Most immigration enforcement in Nevada operates through the Las Vegas Metropolitan Police Department's 287(g) agreement with ICE, which the ACLU of Nevada sued in October 2025 for violating state law. Nevada's Democratic congressional delegation voted against DHS funding to demand ICE reforms, while Republican Rep. Mark Amodei supported it, as protesters call for ICE abolition and lawmakers debate "guardrails" including warrant requirements and body cameras. LV Defensa conducts neighborhood and business outreach, distributing "Know Your Rights" materials and training volunteers to document ICE activity, confirm sightings, and fundraise for affected families.

headline: DACA recipient detained, denied bond hearing by ICE; ACLU Nebraska files suit
summary: The Trump administration's policy directive mandating detention without bond for most immigrants facing deportation—despite a federal court ruling that the practice is unlawful—has led ICE to detain Joel Angel-Becerril, a DACA recipient with work authorization until 2027, without a bond hearing; the ACLU of Nebraska filed a lawsuit arguing this violates the Immigration and Nationality Act and the recent class action judgment, marking its third immigration enforcement case in two weeks.

headline: Weeks Medical Center controversy puts North Country health care system under microscope
summary: The New Hampshire Department of Justice's Charitable Trusts Unit is formally reviewing North Country Healthcare amid patient complaints about care quality, primary care provider shortages, and governance concerns. The review follows a decade after approving the rural hospital system's merger. Critics question whether the system has maintained hospital independence while executive compensation increased 121% and management expenses grew as a share of hospital budgets. The system defends its model as necessary to prevent rural hospital closures.

headline: Activist Analilia Mejia, ex-Rep. Tom Malinowski leading in special Dem primary
summary: Progressive candidate Analilia Mejia's election-night rhetoric attacking oligarchs and calling to reclaim democracy, her policy stance on conditioning Israel aid that provoked AIPAC-affiliated opposition, and the tightly contested primary exposing deep ideological splits within the Democratic Party, all represent political flashpoints and grievances that frequently catalyze protests and civil unrest.

headline: Gov. Healey’s budget plan is  spending Massachusetts into a corner
summary: Massachusetts faces a widening structural deficit as spending grows faster than tax revenues, with the Healey administration relying on $4.7 billion in supplemental spending and one-time accounting maneuvers to balance the budget, while misusing the income tax surcharge meant for infrastructure to cover operating costs, amid poor economic growth that has cost the state nearly 12,000 private-sector jobs and approximately $1 billion in annual tax revenue, prompting warnings of a potential $3 billion deficit if current trends continue.

headline: Georgia House lawmakers go for property tax break instead of governor’s income tax rebate
summary: The Georgia House passed a $42.3 billion revised budget that replaces Gov. Kemp's $1.2 billion income tax rebate proposal with $850 million in homeowner property tax relief grants, fully funds an $85 million deficit at the Division of Family and Children Services, and advances to the Senate where Republican leaders are pushing for income tax elimination instead, creating a significant policy divide over the state's surplus allocation.

headline: Longtime human rights commissioner fired by governor after social media posts critical of ICE
summary: Idaho Human Rights Commission member Estella Zamora was terminated after Gov. Brad Little withdrew her re-appointment following criticism of her social media posts condemning ICE actions, including reposts about Minneapolis protests where agents shot two people and an AI image questioning deportation policies. Senate President Pro Tempore Kelly Anthon opposed her appointment, stating commission members must be committed to "rule of law" and blind enforcement without letting personal beliefs compromise that position.

headline: Michigan Court of Appeals rejects state’s attempt to appeal work projects spending injunction
summary: The Michigan Court of Appeals denied immediate review of an injunction that bars spending $349.5 million in unobligated funds from $645 million in work project cuts by House Republicans, perpetuating a state budget dispute. Attorney General Dana Nessel deemed the cuts unconstitutional, while House Speaker Matt Hall is suing to enforce the reductions and eliminate what he calls Democratic "slush funds," leaving these appropriations frozen pending further litigation.

headline: Early childhood educators urge New Mexico Senate to keep wage increase in budget
summary: New Mexico's $11 billion budget bill (HB2) includes a $60 million allocation for early childhood education worker raises that advocates are urging the Senate Finance Committee to preserve, while Gov. Michelle Lujan Grisham opposes the Legislature's co-pay provision for higher-earning families as inconsistent with her universal childcare vision, with negotiations ongoing between the governor and lawmakers.

headline: Effort to require elections for excess school taxation falters in state House
summary: The South Dakota House defeated a bill (29-39) that would have required mandatory elections when school boards opt out of property tax limitations, maintaining the petition-based referendum system for K-12 funding increases. The proposal, which had passed the Senate 20-14, would have forced automatic voter approval after a two-thirds board vote and required districts to specify tax duration and total amounts. Legislative debate revealed a policy divide: supporters championed taxpayer transparency and consent, while opponents argued the measure would constrain school district authority and highlighted inadequate state K-12 funding as the cause of opt-outs. The House sponsor's decision not to pursue reconsideration effectively killed the legislation, which critics including Americans for Prosperity viewed as a missed opportunity for property tax oversight. The outcome preserves current school board power to raise taxes above state limits with citizen petition as the only electoral check.

headline: Gov. Kim Reynolds proposes raising tobacco taxes amid high cancer rates
summary: Gov. Kim Reynolds introduced legislation to increase taxes on cigarettes, tobacco, hemp, and vape products while restricting SNAP purchases of candy/soft drinks, eliminating food dyes from school lunches, and requiring physician nutrition education, all aimed at addressing Iowa's second-highest age-adjusted cancer rate in the nation. The Iowa Department of Health and Human Services and University of Iowa College of Public Health are conducting a $1 million research project that identified lung, prostate, breast, melanoma, and colorectal cancers as primary drivers of Iowa's statistically significant rise in cancer rates since 2013-2014, with 2,582 more Iowans diagnosed in 2022 than national rates would predict. State Medical Director Dr. Robert Kruse emphasized behavioral interventions including radon testing, tobacco cessation, alcohol limitation, and skin protection, while announcing the "Healthy Hometowns" rural health plan funded by $209 million in federal Rural Health Transformation Program funds to expand cancer screening, radon mitigation, and specialized care access in rural areas through a hub-and-spoke model. Researchers will complete environmental and genetic analysis in the project's second year and deliver evidence-based prevention recommendations to lawmakers.

headline: Hopes dim for quick resolution to Homeland Security funding bill
summary: Connecticut Democratic lawmakers, including Sens. Chris Murphy and Richard Blumenthal and Rep. Rosa DeLauro, are central to negotiations over DHS funding and proposed reforms to ICE enforcement practices. Democrats are demanding warrant requirements for private property entry, restrictions on enforcement near schools and religious institutions, an end to racial profiling, and mandatory body cameras for agents. Republicans, led by Sen. Katie Britt, have rejected these proposals as a "ridiculous Christmas list" and are pushing for crackdowns on sanctuary cities instead. With DHS funding set to expire February 13, failure to reach agreement threatens a partial government shutdown that would impact ICE, FEMA, TSA, and the Coast Guard. Negotiations are stalled by confusion over which Republican leaders—whether Senate Majority Leader Thune, the White House, or Britt—Democrats should engage with to finalize a deal.

headline: Ellis demands redistricting vote, walks off Senate floor in protest
summary: Sen. Arthur Ellis (D-Charles) protested Thursday by leaving the Senate floor and vowing to boycott floor activity for the remaining two months of the legislative session until House Bill 488, a congressional redistricting measure, receives a full vote. Ellis refused to register his presence for quorum calls to pressure Senate President Bill Ferguson (D-Baltimore City), who assigned the bill to the Rules Committee where it is unlikely to advance. The protest exposes internal Democratic divisions over midcycle redistricting, with Gov. Wes Moore and House leadership supporting the bill while Ferguson controls the Senate agenda. While potentially disruptive, Ellis's action may be largely symbolic given Democrats hold 34 of 47 seats and only 24 members are needed for quorum. Ellis dared leadership to retaliate, framing his protest as defending democracy and protecting Black and brown communities from federal actions.
